title: Wood Seesaw (Teeter-Totter)
summary: A backyard seesaw with a bolt pivot, dowel handles, and padded seats — plus three pivot heights, so it grows with the kids instead of aging out.
description:
A seesaw is a lever with a liability attached, so this build spends its effort exactly where it should: the pivot. The beam is two 8-foot 2x4s sandwiched around the handle supports — a laminated beam far stiffer than any single board — swinging on a 5/8" bolt through a pair of 2x6 uprights on a wide cross-shaped base. Washers ride each side of the uprights so the beam pivots on steel, not on wood grinding wood.

The smartest feature costs three extra holes: the uprights are drilled at three heights, so the pivot moves up as the kids grow — and moving the bolt to a lower hole makes the ride gentler for the small ones. The source builders are honest that aligning the bolt holes through four thicknesses is the hardest part; their fix (drill the holes a touch oversize so the bolt passes without binding) is correct, and it also makes the height changes practical.

Seats are 1x8 offcuts padded with a foam chair pad halved and wrapped in outdoor fabric; handles are closet-rod dowel glued and screwed into drilled holes. Set it on grass, not concrete — the ground under a seesaw is part of the safety equipment.

tools.note: Includes the 30° cuts on the short base pieces.
The 5/8" pivot holes and the 1" handle holes — the precision work of the build.
Persuading the bolt through four thicknesses.
This is playground equipment — every edge and the whole beam get sanded.

materials.name: 2x6, 8 ft
materials.note: Uprights, base, and handle supports — cut one of each length from each board to conserve stock.

materials.name: Bolt, 5/8" x 10", with nut and four washers
materials.note: The pivot. Washers on BOTH sides of each upright — steel bears on steel, not wood.

materials.name: Foam chair pad and outdoor fabric
materials.note: One pad, cut in half — two seats.

cutList.note: Drilled at three heights for the adjustable pivot.
Both ends 30° off square, NOT parallel — the splayed feet.

steps.title: Build the base
steps.body: Screw and glue the long base boards to the uprights — 'lots of screws' is the technical specification, and it's right: this junction takes the full alternating load of two launching children. Keep the uprights dead parallel to each other; the beam has to swing between them.

steps.title: Add the splayed feet
steps.body: Attach the 30°-cut short base pieces as side supports, again with screws and glue everywhere. The splay is the anti-tip geometry — the seesaw's side-to-side stability all lives here.

steps.title: Laminate the beam
steps.body:
Sandwich the two handle supports between the two 8-foot 2x4s — one support a seat-width from each end — with glue and screws driven from both faces in a staggered pattern.

Then drill the 5/8" pivot hole through the dead center of the beam. Measure twice: an off-center pivot builds a seesaw that favors one kid forever.

steps.title: Drill the uprights, hang the beam, finish the fun parts
steps.body:
Drill three 5/8" holes up each upright at matching heights — the height options. Drill them a touch oversize (the source's hard-won advice): the bolt must pass through upright-beam-upright without a hammer fight, or you'll never actually use the adjustability.

Hang the beam: washer, upright, washer, beam, washer, upright, washer, nut. Snug, not crushing — it's a bearing, not a clamp. Screw and glue the padded seats over the beam ends, drill 1" holes through the handle supports, and glue and screw the dowel handles in.

Sand everything a child touches, set it on grass, and enforce the one seesaw rule: nobody dismounts without warning the other end.
materials: Bolt, 5/8" x 10", with nut and four washers
Foam chair pad and outdoor fabric

images.alt: Backyard wooden seesaw with padded seats and dowel handles on a cross base
